title: Basis of payment - Cost reimbursable | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Importance of a clear procurement requirement
Completing the Security Requirements Check List
Security in standing offers and supply arrangements
Compliance with the Canadian electrical code

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: Cost reimbursable

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: A cost reimbursable basis of payment reimburses the contractor for allowable costs incurred during the performance of the contract. You should use this type of basis of payment primarily when the scope and requirements of the work are uncertain.

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: Cost reimbursable pricing is suitable for negotiated contracts but may not motivate a contractor to limit costs unless paired with a cost control incentive. These incentives, in a cost reimbursable with a target cost basis of payment, are described in section 4.1.3 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB).

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: The appropriate scenarios for cost reimbursable pricing include:

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: research and development

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: major system development

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: prototype development and testing

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: low rate initial production

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: immature industry, platform, service or product

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: minimal competition

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: poorly defined concepts or understanding of requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: Conditions for using cost reimbursable basis of payment are as follows:

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: the contractor’s accounting system is adequate for determining costs applicable to the contract

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: adequate government resources are in place to manage the contract which includes reviewing and validating costs claimed

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: appropriate validation mechanisms are in place both during contract performance and after contract completion ensuring efficient methods and cost controls are used

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: it is not possible to reasonably estimate a price for the work that both Canada and the contractor can agree on for equitable sharing of risks and responsibilities

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: a commercial price is unavailable

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable
structure_blocks.text: Refer to Contract Cost Principles for a description of allowable costs.

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
structure_blocks.text: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
structure_blocks.text: This basis of payment involves gain or pain sharing, where cost efficiencies or losses are rewarded and shared through fee arrangements in which both the contractor and Canada share the rewards or risks of meeting or not meeting contract performance criteria.

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
structure_blocks.text: Use this basis of payment when a fixed or firm price basis of payment is not suitable, and the nature of the goods or services justifies a shared financial responsibility incentivizing the contractor to manage costs and contract performance effectively.

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
structure_blocks.text: There are 2 main types of target cost/incentive fee basis of payment:

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
structure_blocks.text: with no maximum price, where both parties share in cost savings or costs exceeding the target in the performance of the contract, with no limit to the losses or gains incurred

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
structure_blocks.text: with maximum price, where both parties share in cost savings or costs exceeding the target in the performance of the contract up to a set maximum price

Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
structure_blocks.text: It may be necessary to negotiate in advance a target cost, target fee, maximum fee and a formula for fee adjustment in advance, depending on the type of target cost incentive fee basis of payment.

structure_blocks.heading: Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
structure_blocks.text: The target cost should be the estimated costs of performing the work computed in accordance with costing principles and standards of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB). The target fee and maximum fee should be no greater than that calculated in accordance with Contract Cost Principles and Costing Standard in section 5.2 of Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB).

structure_blocks.heading: Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
structure_blocks.text: When setting targets and percentages for gain/pain sharing formulas you should assess the likelihood of deviations from the established target. Actual experience may indicate that changes to the contractor’s underlying business solution or Canada’s requirements could necessitate amending the sharing agreement and the contract.

structure_blocks.heading: Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
structure_blocks.text: The sharing formula provides for:

structure_blocks.heading: Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
structure_blocks.text: an increase in fee above the target, up to the maximum fee, for actual acceptable costs below the target

structure_blocks.heading: Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee
Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment
structure_blocks.text: a reduction of the fee below the target, for actual acceptable costs above the target

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
structure_blocks.text: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
structure_blocks.text: This basis of payment option reimburses the contractor for the actual costs incurred in the performance of the contract. The fixed fee remains unchanged regardless of the actual costs incurred but you may renegotiate under certain circumstances. Use this approach when a fixed price basis of payment is unsuitable and where the possible savings from a cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee contract are likely offset by the complexities of contract administration. This is used primarily for research and advanced development or in projects where the required effort level is unknown.

structure_blocks.heading: Determining a fixed fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
structure_blocks.text: Determining a fixed fee

structure_blocks.heading: Determining a fixed fee
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
structure_blocks.text: The fixed fee is based on estimated costs to be incurred and should not exceed reasonable profit. If there is difficulty in agreeing on the estimated cost, use swing points as a basis for calculating the fixed fee. Swing points refer to estimated costs thresholds, one higher and one lower than the amount used for the calculation of the fixed fee. If costs move beyond these points, the fixed fee will be renegotiated. Refer to the section 4.1.3.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB) for guidance.

structure_blocks.heading: Fixed fee and ceiling price
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
structure_blocks.text: Fixed fee and ceiling price

structure_blocks.heading: Fixed fee and ceiling price
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee
structure_blocks.text: In a cost reimbursable contract (or part of a contract) with a fixed fee and a ceiling price, the contractor must complete the prescribed work without additional payment, even if actual costs exceed the ceiling price. When a contract does not include a ceiling price, limitation of expenditure must be used. A clear agreement on what constitutes prescribed work is essential and you must provide advance approval for any changes or additional work by the contractor.

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with fee based on actual costs
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with fee based on actual costs
structure_blocks.text: This basis of payment, which compensates the contractor based on actual costs incurred plus a fixed percentage fee, is not recommended because it provides little or no costs control and actually encourages contractors to raise costs to increase profit. Use this basis of payment only when no other basis of payment are available. Section 4.1.3.4 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB) provides further guidance on the decision process for this basis of payment type.

heading_path: Cost reimbursable with fee based on actual costs

structure_blocks.heading: Calculating fee based on actual costs
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with fee based on actual costs
Calculating fee based on actual costs
structure_blocks.text: The amount of fee is based on actual costs incurred, as determined by the contract cost principles and/or the costing standard in section 4.1.3.4 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB). The total fee should be no greater than the appropriate level of profit. Costs and fees are subject to government audit.

structure_blocks.heading: Calculating fee based on actual costs
heading_path: Cost reimbursable with fee based on actual costs
Calculating fee based on actual costs
structure_blocks.text: Ceiling prices are not applicable when this basis of payment is used, as the fee is based on actual costs incurred. Since the fee is based on actual costs incurred, it is important to validate the costs claimed by the contractor upon completion of the contract or periodically, for example annually in the case of multi-year contracts.

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with no fee
structure_blocks.text: Cost reimbursable with no fee

structure_blocks.heading: Cost reimbursable with no fee
structure_blocks.text: This basis of payment reimburses the contractor solely for actual costs incurred, without any additional fee for profit, as may be determined by government audit. It is primarily used in contracts that assist the contractor and is rarely employed due to the lack of profitability for the contractor. You may refer to section 4.1.3.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB) for further guidance.

structure_blocks.heading: Exclude ceiling prices
structure_blocks.text: Exclude ceiling prices

structure_blocks.heading: Exclude ceiling prices
structure_blocks.text: A contract, or part of a contract, with a cost reimbursable with no fee basis of payment should not include a ceiling price. The intent is to cover actual costs without profit. Instead, a limitation of expenditure clause must be included in the contract.

structure_blocks.text: Since the price is based on actual costs incurred, it is important to validate the costs claimed by the contractor through audit upon completion of the contract or periodically, for example in the case of multi-year contracts.

structure_blocks.heading: Incorporating contractual clauses
structure_blocks.text: a limitation of expenditure clause for:

structure_blocks.heading: Incorporating contractual clauses
structure_blocks.text: cost reimbursable contracts with target cost/incentive fee basis of payment with no maximum rice

structure_blocks.heading: Incorporating contractual clauses
structure_blocks.text: a ceiling price clause for contracts with a cost reimbursable target cost/incentive fee basis of payment with a maximum price

structure_blocks.heading: Incorporating contractual clauses

raw_text:
Cost reimbursable

A cost reimbursable basis of payment reimburses the contractor for allowable costs incurred during the performance of the contract. You should use this type of basis of payment primarily when the scope and requirements of the work are uncertain.

Cost reimbursable pricing is suitable for negotiated contracts but may not motivate a contractor to limit costs unless paired with a cost control incentive. These incentives, in a cost reimbursable with a target cost basis of payment, are described in section 4.1.3 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB).

The appropriate scenarios for cost reimbursable pricing include:

research and development

major system development

prototype development and testing

low rate initial production

immature industry, platform, service or product

minimal competition

poorly defined concepts or understanding of requirements

Conditions for using cost reimbursable basis of payment are as follows:

the contractor’s accounting system is adequate for determining costs applicable to the contract

adequate government resources are in place to manage the contract which includes reviewing and validating costs claimed

appropriate validation mechanisms are in place both during contract performance and after contract completion ensuring efficient methods and cost controls are used

it is not possible to reasonably estimate a price for the work that both Canada and the contractor can agree on for equitable sharing of risks and responsibilities

a commercial price is unavailable

Refer to Contract Cost Principles for a description of allowable costs.

Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee

This basis of payment involves gain or pain sharing, where cost efficiencies or losses are rewarded and shared through fee arrangements in which both the contractor and Canada share the rewards or risks of meeting or not meeting contract performance criteria.

Use this basis of payment when a fixed or firm price basis of payment is not suitable, and the nature of the goods or services justifies a shared financial responsibility incentivizing the contractor to manage costs and contract performance effectively.

There are 2 main types of target cost/incentive fee basis of payment:

with no maximum price, where both parties share in cost savings or costs exceeding the target in the performance of the contract, with no limit to the losses or gains incurred

with maximum price, where both parties share in cost savings or costs exceeding the target in the performance of the contract up to a set maximum price

Setting up the target cost/incentive fee basis of payment

It may be necessary to negotiate in advance a target cost, target fee, maximum fee and a formula for fee adjustment in advance, depending on the type of target cost incentive fee basis of payment.

The target cost should be the estimated costs of performing the work computed in accordance with costing principles and standards of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB). The target fee and maximum fee should be no greater than that calculated in accordance with Contract Cost Principles and Costing Standard in section 5.2 of Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB).

When setting targets and percentages for gain/pain sharing formulas you should assess the likelihood of deviations from the established target. Actual experience may indicate that changes to the contractor’s underlying business solution or Canada’s requirements could necessitate amending the sharing agreement and the contract.

The sharing formula provides for:

an increase in fee above the target, up to the maximum fee, for actual acceptable costs below the target

a reduction of the fee below the target, for actual acceptable costs above the target

Cost reimbursable with a fixed fee

This basis of payment option reimburses the contractor for the actual costs incurred in the performance of the contract. The fixed fee remains unchanged regardless of the actual costs incurred but you may renegotiate under certain circumstances. Use this approach when a fixed price basis of payment is unsuitable and where the possible savings from a cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee contract are likely offset by the complexities of contract administration. This is used primarily for research and advanced development or in projects where the required effort level is unknown.

Determining a fixed fee

The fixed fee is based on estimated costs to be incurred and should not exceed reasonable profit. If there is difficulty in agreeing on the estimated cost, use swing points as a basis for calculating the fixed fee. Swing points refer to estimated costs thresholds, one higher and one lower than the amount used for the calculation of the fixed fee. If costs move beyond these points, the fixed fee will be renegotiated. Refer to the section 4.1.3.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB) for guidance.

Fixed fee and ceiling price

In a cost reimbursable contract (or part of a contract) with a fixed fee and a ceiling price, the contractor must complete the prescribed work without additional payment, even if actual costs exceed the ceiling price. When a contract does not include a ceiling price, limitation of expenditure must be used. A clear agreement on what constitutes prescribed work is essential and you must provide advance approval for any changes or additional work by the contractor.

Cost reimbursable with fee based on actual costs

This basis of payment, which compensates the contractor based on actual costs incurred plus a fixed percentage fee, is not recommended because it provides little or no costs control and actually encourages contractors to raise costs to increase profit. Use this basis of payment only when no other basis of payment are available. Section 4.1.3.4 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB) provides further guidance on the decision process for this basis of payment type.

Calculating fee based on actual costs

The amount of fee is based on actual costs incurred, as determined by the contract cost principles and/or the costing standard in section 4.1.3.4 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB). The total fee should be no greater than the appropriate level of profit. Costs and fees are subject to government audit.

Ceiling prices are not applicable when this basis of payment is used, as the fee is based on actual costs incurred. Since the fee is based on actual costs incurred, it is important to validate the costs claimed by the contractor upon completion of the contract or periodically, for example annually in the case of multi-year contracts.

Cost reimbursable with no fee

This basis of payment reimburses the contractor solely for actual costs incurred, without any additional fee for profit, as may be determined by government audit. It is primarily used in contracts that assist the contractor and is rarely employed due to the lack of profitability for the contractor. You may refer to section 4.1.3.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing (PDF, 6.50 MB) for further guidance.

Exclude ceiling prices

A contract, or part of a contract, with a cost reimbursable with no fee basis of payment should not include a ceiling price. The intent is to cover actual costs without profit. Instead, a limitation of expenditure clause must be included in the contract.

Validate costs claimed

Since the price is based on actual costs incurred, it is important to validate the costs claimed by the contractor through audit upon completion of the contract or periodically, for example in the case of multi-year contracts.

Incorporating contractual clauses

When using cost reimbursable basis of payments, you must include appropriate clauses related to cost limits and audit in the contract. Specifically, you must include:

a limitation of expenditure clause for:

cost reimbursable contracts with target cost/incentive fee basis of payment with no maximum rice

cost reimbursable contracts with fee based on actual costs

cost reimbursable contracts with no fee

cost reimbursable contracts without a ceiling price

a ceiling price clause for contracts with a cost reimbursable target cost/incentive fee basis of payment with a maximum price

the appropriate audit clause in all cost reimbursable contracts to enable periodic validation of costs, ensuring compliance and accountability throughout the contract duration

For guidance on the inclusion of audit clauses, refer to Audit .
